title: Fields
html:
Fields

  • Last UpdatedSept 23, 2024

    • 10.4

Fields

You can use fields as operands in expressions. The ProcScript interpreter understands that the field name refers to the contents of the field, not the field name itself.

For example, the following expression checks whether the field SURNAME contains the data CREW:

if ( SURNAME = "CREW" )
message "Hi, %%FIRSTNAME"
endif

Qualifying Field Names

When a field in a component is specified in a ProcScript statement, Uniface searches for the field in the current entity first. If the field is not in the current entity, Uniface searches for the field from the outermost entity in the component down to the lowest innermost.

If the same field name is used in more than one entity on a component (as is often the case with foreign key fields), you can specify which one you want to access by qualifying it with the name of the entity. The format for qualifying fields is:

field.entity

The following example shows ProcScript statements that copy a value to two fields which have the same name, but are in different entities:

DEPTHEAD.MANAGERS = $1
DEPTHEAD.DEPARTMENT = $2

Indirection in Field References

Indirection of field names allows a ProcScript statement to access the contents of a field without having to refer to the field directly by its specific name. The statement refers instead to an operand which contains the field name. The operand which holds the required field name can be $fieldname, any variable, or another field name.

The symbol which denotes indirection is the at sign (@). If an operand is preceded by @, Uniface understands that the operand following the @ contains the name of the field where the information to be used can be found.

The following example gives some examples of indirection in ProcScript statements. How Uniface interprets each of these examples is displayed here as a comment next to each line of code:

trigger Detail ; in field COMPANY
$1 = "NUM.INVOICE" ;load string into $1
$2 = $fieldname ;load string "COMPANY" into $2
;and then ...
$30 = @$1 ;put contents of NUM.INVOICE into $30
$40 = @$2 ;put contents of COMPANY into $40
$50 = @$fieldname ;put contents of current field into $50
; and more ...
@$1 = "12345" ;put "12345" into NUM.INVOICE
@$fieldname = "" ;empty current field (COMPANY)
end; detail

When using indirection, keep the following points in mind:

  • Only fields can be referenced indirectly; the name reached by the indirect reference cannot be a variable. The following example is incorrect because the indirection refers to a general variable rather than to a field: 
    $1 = "$99"
@$1 = "This string does not go to $99."
  • Indirection always takes priority over extraction. For more information, see Extracting Values From String Data.
  • Indirection of field names is not allowed in combination with Uniface functions such as $fieldmod, $prompt, or $previous. For example, $fieldmod(@$1) is illegal.
  • Uniface is unable to optimize indirect references to fields, except for @$fieldname. This means that any performance improvement that could be gained by the ProcScript compiler through binding of field names is lost.

If indirection is used incorrectly, an empty string ("") is returned and a message indicating the reason for the failure appears on the message line. This error occurs if the field is not available on the component, that is, it has not been painted and it is not included in the field list of the component.
